hero.title: QR Code Generator
hero.description: From URL creation and token mapping to the 302 redirect path, this page breaks down the core trade-offs around uniqueness, caching, and scale in a QR Code service.
hero.targetsNote: Shown as case-study assumptions, not live production metrics

bottleneck.title: The bottleneck is not the QR image
bottleneck.description: See why token design, redirect behavior, and cache strategy together shape the user experience.

apiSurface.title: Cut the API surface cleanly
apiSurface.description: Understand the boundaries between create, image retrieval, original URL lookup, edit, and delete.

scaling.title: Think in terms of 1B codes
scaling.description: Learn why read-heavy systems eventually become an index, cache, CDN, and replica topology problem.

visual.title: QR → token → redirect
visual.description: This diagram is intentionally not a product mock. It foregrounds the request lifecycle and the hottest path first.

redirectLatency.label: Redirect latency
redirectLatency.note: Should stay smooth under hot traffic

qrCodes.label: QR codes
qrCodes.note: Capacity target used in the design exercise

users.label: Users
users.note: Scale assumption from the source material

navigation.mobileTocAriaLabel: QR Code Generator mobile table of contents
navigation.desktopTocAriaLabel: QR Code Generator table of contents

base62.label: Base62
base62.description: A 62-character alphabet [0-9A-Za-z] used to keep tokens short, readable, and easy to share.

collision.label: Collision
collision.description: When different inputs accidentally produce the same token. A UNIQUE constraint plus retry logic is the usual fix.

readHeavy.label: Read-heavy
readHeavy.description: A traffic shape where reads far outnumber writes, making caching and indexing more important.

cdn.label: CDN
cdn.description: An edge delivery layer that serves static assets or hot results closer to users to cut regional latency.

ui.copyUnsupported: This browser does not support copying to the clipboard.
ui.copyFailure: Copy failed. Please select the code manually.

whyHard.title: Why this is hard
whyHard.takeaway: The hard part is not rendering the QR image. It is balancing token uniqueness, redirect freshness, and read-heavy traffic.

requirements.title: Requirements
requirements.takeaway: The system must create, manage, and redirect, which means both the data model and the experience have to stay editable.

apiSurface.title: API surface
apiSurface.takeaway: The API surface should clearly separate create, image retrieval, edit, delete, and original-URL lookup responsibilities.

lifecycle.title: Lifecycle
lifecycle.takeaway: Create and retrieve flows bottleneck in different places: token writes on one side, caching and redirect on the other.

scaling.title: Scaling
scaling.takeaway: Once traffic grows, indexes, caches, CDNs, and read replicas become the real stars, not the QR image itself.

tradeOffs.title: Trade-offs
tradeOffs.takeaway: The core design move is to admit the trade-offs: 302 over 301, UNIQUE index over optimistic guessing, simple DB before distribution.

uniqueness.title: Uniqueness vs. token length
uniqueness.description: The token must stay short enough to share easily while still being globally unique and recoverable after collisions.

freshness.title: Freshness vs. caching
freshness.description: A QR mapping can be edited or deleted later, so the redirect path cannot be cached forever.

readHeavy.title: Read-heavy traffic vs. simple architecture
readHeavy.description: When reads dominate writes, cache hit rate and edge delivery matter more than the write path itself.

functional.title: Functional requirements
functional.item1: Users can submit a URL and receive a generated QR Code.
functional.item2: Authenticated users can manage the QR Codes they created, with ownership derived from JWT claims.
functional.item3: Scanning a QR Code redirects to the original URL.

nonFunctional.title: Non-functional requirements
nonFunctional.item1: The service should remain highly available 24/7.
nonFunctional.item3: The design should reason about 1B QR codes and 100M users.

create.title: Create a QR Code
create.description: Create a new QR Code mapping and return the resulting qr_token.

image.title: Get QR image
image.description: Retrieve the QR image location by qr_token and optional styling parameters.

edit.title: Edit a QR Code
edit.description: Update the original URL behind an existing mapping.

delete.title: Delete a QR Code
delete.description: Delete an existing QR Code mapping.

lookup.title: Get original URL
lookup.description: Read the original URL behind a token for inspection or downstream flows.

receive.title: 1. Receive the create request
receive.description: The client sends a URL to POST /v1/qr_code.

validate.title: 2. Validate the URL and generate a token
validate.description: The backend validates the URL and then generates a globally unique qr_token.

write.title: 3. Write to the database
write.description: Persist the mapping in the QrCodes table and rely on the UNIQUE constraint for collision handling.

return.title: 4. Return the result
return.description: Return the qr_token to the client. The image can be produced later through another API.

enter.title: 1. Enter the read path
enter.description: The client uses qr_token to fetch the image, resolve the original URL, or scan directly into the redirect flow.

cache.title: 2. Check cache / edge first
cache.description: Hot requests should hit cache or CDN first so they avoid the database whenever possible.

lookup.title: 3. Fall back to origin lookup
lookup.description: When a fallback is needed, the backend looks up the mapping through the qr_token index.

redirect.title: 4. Execute the 302 redirect
redirect.description: Use a 302 redirect so that mapping updates still reach the latest target.

indexing.title: Indexing
indexing.item1: qr_token needs an index to avoid full table scans.
indexing.item2: The UNIQUE constraint also doubles as collision detection.

caching.title: Caching
caching.item1: This is a read-heavy service, with write:read around 1:100.
caching.item2: Only hit the database on cache miss, and invalidate on delete.

cdn.title: CDN
cdn.item1: QR images are natural static assets for edge delivery.
cdn.item2: Popular QR Codes can be served directly from the CDN to cut cross-region latency.

replicas.title: Replicas & cleanup
replicas.item1: A single DB instance is enough for the foreseeable future, but read replicas should be planned.
replicas.item2: Idle mappings can be cleaned up with cron jobs to avoid long-term bloat.

redirect.reason: This avoids permanent browser caching so edits and deletes still resolve to the latest mapping state.

token.decision: Use hash + nonce + Base62 instead of a purely deterministic hash
token.reason: This avoids giving identical inputs identical tokens forever and preserves more entropy and flexibility.

database.decision: Start with one primary DB and extend with read replicas
database.reason: At roughly 200GB of data, the architecture can stay simple first and push complexity to when it is truly needed.

edge.decision: Push QR images and hot mappings to CDN / cache
edge.reason: The hottest path is read and redirect, not write. Edge hits cut latency dramatically.

takeaways.item1: The heart of a QR Code generator is not drawing text as an image. It is turning URL mapping into a readable, editable, cacheable service.
takeaways.item2: 302 redirects, UNIQUE indexes, and cache / CDN hit rates are the three main design levers in this case study.
takeaways.item3: This page presents the system-design thinking, not a live tool wired to a backend.

footer.sourceNote: This page reorganizes the user-provided PDF into a Hans-OS system-design reading experience.
